text: Cancer-associated fibroblasts(CAFs) are a heterogeneous cell population that plays a crucial role in remodeling the tumor microenvironment(TME). Here, through the integrated analysis of spatial and single-cell transcriptomics data across six common cancer types, we identified four distinct functional subgroups of CAFs and described their spatial distribution characteristics. Additionally, the analysis of single-cell RNA sequencing(scRNA-seq) data from three additional common cancer types and two newly generated scRNA-seq datasets of rare cancer types, namely epithelial-myoepithelial carcinoma(EMC) and mucoepidermoid carcinoma(MEC), expanded our understanding of CAF heterogeneity. Cell-cell interaction analysis conducted within the spatial context highlighted the pivotal roles of matrix CAFs(mCAFs) in tumor angiogenesis and inflammatory CAFs(iCAFs) in shaping the immunosuppressive microenvironment. In patients with breast cancer(BRCA) undergoing anti-PD-1 immunotherapy, iCAFs demonstrated heightened capacity in facilitating cancer cell proliferation, promoting epithelial-mesenchymal transition(EMT), and contributing to the establishment of an immunosuppressive microenvironment. Furthermore, a scoring system based on iCAFs showed a significant correlation with immune therapy response in melanoma patients. Lastly, we provided a web interface(https://chenxisd.shinyapps.io/pancaf/) for the research community to investigate CAFs in the context of pan-cancer.

Tumors display extensive heterogeneity, with cancer cells engaging in reciprocal interactions with their microenvironment, forming a complex ecosystem. Cancer-associated fibroblasts(CAFs), as one of the most prominent cell populations in the tumor microenvironment(TME), have garnered significant attention in recent years. CAF's intricate interactions with stromal components and immune cells play a crucial role in orchestrating TME reorganization, encompassing processes such as angiogenesis, extracellular matrix(ECM) remodeling, and immune evasion. At present, the crucial role of CAFs has been largely overlooked by most therapies, including immunotherapy and chemotherapy. Our current understanding of the interplay between CAFs and components of TME is insufficient to support the development of reliable treatment strategies. Recent applications of single-cell transcriptomics have unraveled the heterogeneity of CAFs within many cancer types. Furthermore, unbiased studies based on single-cell RNA sequencing(scRNA-seq) explored the heterogeneity and plasticity of CAFs from a pan-cancer perspective. Although scRNA-seq provides an opportunity to systematically dissect the heterogeneity of CAFs, the loss of spatial information during tissue dissociation hinders the study of the crosstalk between CAFs and TME. Recently developed spatial transcriptomics(ST) can obtain whole-transcriptome data within tissue sections, thereby preserving the spatial position information of cells. Therefore, orthogonal integration of scRNA-seq data and ST data will help determine the spatial distribution characteristics of CAFs and further dissect the cellular communication between CAFs and TME.

To establish a spatial single-cell landscape in pan-cancer, we acquired scRNA-seq data from 69 samples of 56 patients diagnosed with one of six prevalent cancer types, along with ST data from 22 tissue slices of 22 patients. The data included six types of cancer: BRCA, colorectal cancer(CRC), liver hepatocellular carcinoma(LIHC), ovarian cancer(OVCA), prostate adenocarcinoma(PRAD), and uterine corpus endometrial carcinoma(UCEC). After strict quality control and filtration, a total of 163,919 cells in the scRNA-seq data and 59,529 spots in ST data were retained. We applied CellTrek to quality-controlled scRNA-seq and ST data in pan-cancer to reconstruct spatial single-cell atlases. Even without corresponding scRNA-seq data from the same patient, ST datasets were still largely covered by scRNA-seq datasets based on the co-embedding results. Due to some cells being repeatedly mapped, we ultimately obtained a pan-cancer spatial single-cell transcriptomic atlas containing 744,289 cells.

The reclustering of the fibroblast cluster identified four CAF subtypes, as well as pericytes and smooth muscle cells(SMCs). CFD+fibroblasts showed high expression of chemokines(CCL11, CXCL12, CXCL14) similar to previously reported iCAFs. POSTN+fibroblasts showed high expression of ECM remodeling genes(MMP11, CTHRC1, COL11A1) consistent with matrix CAFs(mCAFs). A cluster related to hypoxia response and glycolysis resembled metabolic CAFs(meCAFs), while proliferative CAFs(pCAFs) exhibited high expression of cell cycle genes(CENPF, NUSAP1). SCENIC analysis revealed distinct regulons: CDX2 in iCAFs, TCF12 in mCAFs, KLF16 in meCAFs, and cell-cycle-related regulons(MYBL2, E2F2) in pCAFs. SCPA analysis showed meCAFs enriched in glycolysis and pyruvate metabolism, while mCAFs showed activity in fatty acid biosynthesis.

Spatial analysis revealed an exclusionary phenomenon between high-density areas of iCAFs and mCAFs. Spatial trajectory analysis indicated a gradual change in cell proportions and biological processes from iCAF-rich to mCAF-rich regions. Using Robust Rank Aggregation(RRA), we found that all CAF subtypes were spatially proximate to each other, with endothelial cells and pericytes enriched near CAFs. Slingshot analysis suggested a potential transition pathway from pericytes to iCAFs, with genes related to wound healing, ECM, and inflammation activated along this trajectory. This suggests that the state of CAFs could be influenced by the specific TME.

Using SpaTalk, we explored the interplay between mCAFs and endothelial cells, finding ligands involved in angiogenesis(VEGFA, VEGFB, VEGFC, PGF, THBS1) binding to receptors(FLT1, KDR, NRP1, ITGB1, ITGA9) on endothelial cells. iCAFs interacted with macrophages via TGFB1/2/3 and CSF1, potentially driving M2 macrophage polarization. iCAFs also interacted with CD8+ T cells via LGALS1(PTPRC), MIF(CXCR4), and TGFB1, potentially inducing T cell exhaustion and apoptosis. Analysis of BRCA patients treated with anti-PD-1 showed that while iCAF proportions didn't change, their transcriptional profiles shifted, upregulating CHI3L1 and genes related to TNFa signaling, EMT, and complement activation.

We constructed an iCAF score using the top ten marker genes via ssGSEA. In melanoma immunotherapy cohorts, patients with high iCAF scores displayed prolonged overall survival(OS) and progression-free survival(PFS). High iCAF scores correlated with higher percentages of responders to anti-PD-1 and anti-CTLA-4 treatments. Analysis of TCGA data revealed that high iCAF score melanoma patients had lower TMB but higher expression of immune checkpoint genes(PDCD1, CTLA4, LAG3), higher immune scores(ESTIMATE, Roh), cytolytic activity(CYT), and tertiary lymphoid structures(TLS) signatures. These findings indicate that the iCAF score is a valuable tool for predicting patient survival and response to ICB therapy.

Samples were processed using the Human Tumor Dissociation Kit. scRNA-seq libraries were prepared using Chromium Single Cell 3' Reagent v3 kits and sequenced on Illumina NovaSeq 6000. Data were processed by CellRanger and Seurat. Quality control filtered cells with >40,000 UMIs, <500 genes, >5,000 genes, or >20% mitochondrial genes. Doublets were removed using DoubletFinder. Harmony was used for batch correction. Malignant cells were identified using CopyKAT. CellTrek was used to map scRNA-seq data to ST coordinates. Differential expression used Seurat's FindAllMarkers(|log2FC|>1, adj.p.val<0.05). SCENIC analysis used pySCENIC. Trajectory analysis used Slingshot. Cell-cell communication was inferred using SpaTalk and CellChat. Survival analysis used Kaplan-Meier curves and log-rank tests. The iCAF score was calculated using ssGSEA.
